one_line_takeaway: SABER fits a Beta-Binomial model to small-budget jailbreak attempt data and uses a derived asymptotic scaling law to extrapolate large-N Best-of-N attack success rates far more accurately (MAE 1.66 vs. 12.04 for the naive baseline on HarmBench) than naively extrapolating from single-shot success rates.
key_finding: Using n=100 samples per query, SABER-Anchored predicts ASR@1000 on HarmBench with a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 1.66 across the 12 attacker/victim/judge combinations in Table 1, versus MAE 12.04 for the naive baseline -- an 86.2% relative error reduction. Per-combination examples from Table 1: for ADV-LLM vs GPT-4.1-mini under the HarmBench Classifier, ground-truth ASR@1000=75.16, baseline predicts 63.40+/-1.43 (error 11.76), SABER predicts 74.28+/-1.36 (error 0.88); for Text-Augmentation vs GPT-4.1-mini under the HarmBench Classifier, ground truth=92.62, baseline=77.23+/-1.57 (error 15.39), SABER=89.44+/-0.97 (error 3.18). Figure 4 shows SABER-Anchored maintains a 4-6x lower MAE than the baseline across varying n and N (e.g. at N=1000, increasing n from 50 to 200 lowers baseline MAE from 14.1 to 5.5 and SABER's from 2.3 to 1.3; at fixed n=100, baseline MAE rises from 7.8 (N=500) to 9.4 (N=1000) while SABER stays at 1.7-1.8). The paper also demonstrates rank reversal: Text-Augmentation has lower ASR@1 than ADV-LLM against GPT-4.1-mini on HarmBench, but surpasses it by ASR@15 (Figure 2), showing that single-shot robustness rankings do not predict large-budget rankings. Table 4 additionally shows SABER can predict the budget Nτ needed to reach a target risk level (e.g. ground-truth budget for tau=95% under Augmentation/Llama-3.1-8B/HarmBench Classifier is 484, predicted ~466 from n=200).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is SABER (Scaling-Aware Best-of-N Estimation of Risk), a statistical estimator that predicts an LLM's jailbreak Attack Success Rate at large sampling budgets (ASR@N, e.g. N=1000) from a small number of per-query attempts (e.g. n=100). The authors model each harmful query's per-attempt jailbreak success probability theta_i as drawn from a Beta(alpha, beta) distribution (Assumption 3.2), derive the asymptotic behavior of the OR-aggregated Bernoulli outcome under this Beta mixture (Theorem 3.1), and combine it into an analytic scaling law ASR@N ~ 1 - [Gamma(alpha+beta)/Gamma(beta)] * N^{-alpha} (Theorem 3.3), where alpha controls how fast risk amplifies with N. They give a small-N moment-matching correction and an 'anchored' variant of the estimator (SABER-Anchored) that is compared against two other SABER variants (SABER-Plugin, SABER-Fit) and against a naive baseline that extrapolates from observed single-shot success rates via 1-(1-theta_hat)^N (the same functional form used in prior Best-of-N jailbreaking work and in Chen et al. 2021's Pass@N baseline).
threat_model: The threat model, stated explicitly in the paper, is an attacker who automates large-scale parallel sampling: for a harmful query, the attacker issues many (up to N, e.g. N=1000) independent adversarial prompt attempts against a victim LLM and succeeds if any single attempt elicits a harmful response, judged by an automated judge model. The pipeline is formalized as attacker A -> victim V -> judge J producing a binary outcome per attempt. The paper argues single-shot (ASR@1) safety evaluation systematically underestimates risk relative to this repeated-probing adversary, and that exhaustively measuring ASR@N for large N is computationally prohibitive, motivating estimation from small budgets.
limitations: Evaluation is confined to a single dataset (HarmBench, 159 queries), two victim models (one open, one closed source), three attackers, and two judges -- generalization to other harm taxonomies, larger/more diverse model families, or adaptive attackers that react to the defender's evaluation strategy is untested. The scaling law (Theorem 3.3) is asymptotic in N and relies on Gamma-function (Stirling-type) approximations; the paper itself notes the approximation is only reliable for N >= 5-10 in typical parameter regimes and provides a small-N correction, meaning accuracy at very small N or atypical (alpha, beta) regions is not guaranteed without that correction. The Beta-mixture assumption (Assumption 3.2, that per-query success probabilities are drawn i.i.d. from a single Beta(alpha,beta) distribution and that repeated attempts are i.i.d. Bernoulli given theta_i) is a modeling simplification; the paper says it validates this via goodness-of-fit in an appendix section (C.2.1) but that validation was not verified in the main-body pages reviewed here. There is a numeric inconsistency between the abstract (MAE 1.66, 86.2% reduction) and the Introduction (MAE 1.25, 89.6% reduction) for what is described as the same headline result -- Table 1 in the body matches the abstract's numbers (1.66 / 86.2%), suggesting the Introduction figure may be stale from an earlier draft.

quantitative_results: SABER-Anchored MAE for ASR@1000 prediction (n=100): 1.66, vs baseline MAE 12.04 (86.2% relative error reduction) - Table 1
ADV-LLM vs GPT-4.1-mini, HarmBench Classifier: ground truth ASR@1000=75.16; baseline=63.40+/-1.43 (err 11.76); SABER=74.28+/-1.36 (err 0.88)
ADV-LLM vs GPT-4.1-mini, LLM Classifier: ground truth=72.18; baseline=58.87+/-0.56 (err 13.31); SABER=70.04+/-0.52 (err 2.14)
ADV-LLM vs Llama-3.1-8B, HarmBench Classifier: ground truth=100.00; baseline=100.00+/-0.00 (err 0.00); SABER=100.00+/-0.00 (err 0.00)
Text-Augmentation vs GPT-4.1-mini, HarmBench Classifier: ground truth=92.62; baseline=77.23+/-1.57 (err 15.39); SABER=89.44+/-0.97 (err 3.18)
Text-Augmentation vs GPT-4.1-mini, LLM Classifier: ground truth=88.59; baseline=67.04+/-2.11 (err 21.55); SABER=82.54+/-1.71 (err 6.05)
Jailbreak-R1 vs GPT-4.1-mini, HarmBench Classifier: ground truth=99.97; baseline=97.48+/-0.44 (err 2.49); SABER=99.71+/-0.05 (err 0.26)
Figure 4(a): at N=1000, baseline MAE falls from 14.1 (n=50) to 5.5 (n=200); SABER-Anchored MAE falls from 2.3 (n=50) to 1.3 (n=200)
Figure 4(b): at n=100, baseline MAE rises from 7.8 (N=500) to 9.4 (N=1000); SABER-Anchored stays at 1.7 (N=500) to 1.8 (N=1000)
Table 4: ground-truth budget for tau=95% target ASR under Augmentation/Llama-3.1-8B/HarmBench Classifier is Ntau=484, SABER prediction from n=200 is ~466
Introduction text states an inconsistent headline figure of MAE 1.25 and 89.6% error reduction, differing from Table 1's 1.66 / 86.2%
baselines_compared: Naive baseline: extrapolates ASR@N from observed per-query single-shot success rate via 1-(1-theta_hat_i)^N, matching the estimator form used in Hughes et al. (2024) and Shah et al. (2025), and analogous to Chen et al. (2021)'s Pass@N baseline
SABER-Plugin and SABER-Fit (two alternative SABER variants compared against SABER-Anchored in Figure 4c, with SABER-Anchored shown to have the lowest MAE)
